title: How to Access Self-Energy When Every Part Is Activated
researcherTopic: observation without the observer, freedom from conditioning
metaDescription: When your internal system is a battlefield of activated parts, how do you find your center? Learn to access the calm, compassionate leadership of the Self.
imageDescription: A pair of hands gently hold a luminous, golden sphere of light, radiating warmth and energy. The background is soft and out of focus, suggesting a quiet, internal space of healing and integration. The overall feeling is one of peace, connection, and the discovery of an inner resource.
finalH2: The Body as a Gateway, Not a Battlefield
htmlContent:
I've sat with hundreds of people as they describe the internal battlefield ~ the clamor of voices, the pull of competing impulses, the sheer exhaustion of a nervous system that has forgotten what peace even feels like. It’s a particular kind of chaos, one where every part of you seems to be screaming for attention at once, leaving no room for the quiet, steady presence we call the Self. We are told to find our center, but what happens when the very ground beneath our feet has been fractured into a dozen warring territories? The search for a singular, cohesive Self can feel like a fool's errand when the internal field is a cacophony of activation, a storm of survival responses all firing in the name of a threat that has long since passed.

The Illusion of a Singular Self

We inherit a cultural and psychological legacy that speaks of the self as a monolithic entity, a singular “I” that is supposed to be in charge of our thoughts, feelings, and actions. Yet, for anyone who has navigated the labyrinth of complex trauma, this idea feels less like a truth and more like a cruel joke. The experience is not one of singularity, but of multiplicity... a collection of parts, each with its own history, its own beliefs, and its own strategies for survival. There are the young, vulnerable parts holding the pain of the past; the protective parts that have built walls of anger, numbness, or vigilance; and the critical parts that police the entire system, ever-watchful for any misstep. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen that the most genuine shifts occur not when we try to force these parts into a false unity, but when we begin to relate to them with curiosity and compassion. It is a radical re-orientation, moving from a model of internal hierarchy to one of internal community.

This shift requires a fundamental change in how we direct our attention. Instead of being lost in the content of each part’s story... the fear, the shame, the rage ~ we can begin to notice the awareness that is holding it all. The philosopher Jiddu Krishnamurti spoke of observation without the observer, a state of pure witnessing in which the one who is looking is not separate from what is being seen. This is the essence of what we might call Self-energy. It is not another part, but the very field of consciousness in which all parts arise and subside. It is the spaciousness that can be present with the storm without becoming the storm. We don’t need to create this space; we need only to recognize that it is already here, waiting to be inhabited. It is the quiet hum beneath the noise, the stillness beneath the agitation. For those of us who have spent a lifetime mistaking the noise for who we are, this recognition is nothing short of a revolution. We might even find that our trauma responses are not who we are, but what happened to us.

The Tyranny of the Urgent

When our internal system is highly activated, it operates under the tyranny of the urgent. Every part believes its need is the most critical, its fear the most pressing. The firefighter part, for instance, which rushes in to numb overwhelming feelings, is not trying to harm us; it is trying to save us from a pain it believes will be annihilating. The manager part, which micromanages our life with perfectionism, is not trying to make us miserable; it is trying to prevent a repeat of past failures. They are locked in a time loop, responding to an old threat as if it were happening right now, unable to see the long-term consequences of their actions.

Something Pete Walker writes about that doesn't get enough attention - the fawn response. Fight, flight, freeze get all the press. But fawning - that automatic people-pleasing, that compulsive caretaking, that inability to say no - that's a trauma response too. And it's one that often gets rewarded by the world around you.

The fawn part learned early that the only way to be safe was to be useful. To be agreeable. To anticipate other people's needs before they even knew they had them. It's exhausting. And it's invisible, because everyone around you benefits from it. They call you "so nice" and "so thoughtful" and they have no idea that underneath that niceness is a terrified child who learned that their own needs would never be met unless they earned it.

If you're reading this and your chest just tightened, or your eyes got wet, or something in you said "that's me" - I want you to know that the fawn response isn't who you are. It's what you learned to do to survive. And you can learn something different. Not overnight. Not by forcing yourself to be "selfish." But by slowly, gently, learning that your needs matter too. That you're allowed to take up space without earning it first.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link).

For further reading and professional resources, visit SAMHSA's trauma resources.

Here is the thing, though.

The Energetic Signature of Self

One of the most common questions that arises in this work is: how do I know when I’m in Self? It’s a valid question, especially when the territory of our inner world has been so dominated by the more extreme energies of our parts. The answer is not a checklist or a formula, but a felt sense, an energetic signature. The energy of the Self, as described in the Internal Family Systems model, is characterized by what are often called the 8 Cs: calmness, curiosity, clarity, compassion, confidence, courage, creativity, and connectedness. These are not qualities we have to manufacture or force. They are the inherent qualities of our core consciousness, which naturally emerge when our parts feel safe enough to step back and allow the Self to lead. It is a state of being, not a state of doing. It is the quiet confidence of the mountain, which is not disturbed by the weather that passes over it.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link).

The path to accessing this Self-energy is often counter-intuitive. It does not involve striving, but softening. It does not require more effort, but less. It is a process of subtraction, not addition. We are not building a new self, but uncovering the one that has been here all along, buried beneath layers of conditioning, trauma, and protective responses. It is a return to the simplicity of being, a remembrance of the silent, spacious awareness that is our true nature. We might find that the more we try to “find” the Self, the more elusive it becomes. But when we simply create the conditions for it to emerge... through mindfulness, through somatic awareness, through compassionate inquiry ~ it reveals itself, not as a grand, dramatic event, but as a quiet, steady presence that has been with us all along.

The Practice of Unblending

This practice is not always easy. Our protective parts are often wary of the Self, having been in charge for so long. They may flood us with doubt, cynicism, or numbness, testing the Self to see if it is a reliable leader. Our task is to meet this resistance not with frustration, but with compassion. We can say to the skeptical part, “I understand why you don’t trust this. You’ve had to do everything on your own. I’m not going to force you to step aside. I’m just going to stay here with you.” It turns out to be the slow, patient work of building an internal secure attachment, showing our parts through consistent action that the Self can be trusted. This is a core component of healing, far more than simply reparenting the inner child; it is about creating a functional, loving internal system.

Here's something that changed everything for me. The idea that I'm not one unified self. That the part of me that wants connection and the part that pushes everyone away - they're both me. They're both trying to help. They just have very different strategies.

Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model gave language to something I'd felt my whole life but couldn't name. Those contradictions inside you? The way you want intimacy but sabotage every relationship? The way you crave rest but can't stop working? Those aren't character flaws. Those are parts. And every single one of them has a reason for existing.

Think about the part of you that's reading this right now. Is it curious? Skeptical? Hopeful? Afraid? Whatever it is, can you acknowledge it without trying to change it? That's the beginning of parts work. Not fixing. Not silencing. Just acknowledging. "I see you. I hear you. You make sense." Wild how powerful those words can be when directed inward.

The Architecture of Returning

Accessing Self-energy is not a destination we arrive at, but a continuous process of returning. We will get blended with our parts again and again. We will get lost in the storms of our own nervous systems. It turns out to be not a sign of failure; it is the nature of being human, especially for those of us with a history of complex trauma. The measure of our practice is not whether we get lost, but how quickly we can find our way back. It is the muscle of remembrance, the capacity to recognize, “Ah, I am blended with my anxious part again,” and to gently, without judgment, guide our attention back to the seat of the Self. Each time we do this, we strengthen the neural pathways of Self-leadership. We are, in a very real sense, rewiring our brains for compassion and presence.

A simple supplement that many find supports their mood is Garden of Life Vitamin D3 5000 IU (paid link).

Ultimately, the work to accessing Self-energy is one of real trust ~ trust in our own capacity to heal, trust in the inherent wisdom of our own system, and trust in the spacious, compassionate nature of awareness itself. It is a work that takes us from the fractured, warring terrain of our parts to the unified field of the Self. It is a process home. It is not about banishing our parts or getting rid of our pain, but about creating a large enough container to hold it all with love. It is the understanding that every part has a place, every story has a voice, and at the center of it all is the silent, unwavering presence of the Self, which has been waiting patiently for us to return.

Let that land for a moment.

The Body as a Gateway, Not a Battlefield

For many who have endured complex trauma, the body feels less like a home and more like a source of constant betrayal ~ a repository of unprocessed fear, a terrain of chronic tension, a battlefield of symptoms that make no logical sense. We learn to live in our heads, treating the body as a machine to be managed, ignored, or even punished. Yet, it is precisely through this devalued and disconnected body that the most direct path to Self-energy can be found. The body is not the enemy; it is the instrument of our incarnation, the physical appearance of our consciousness, and the gateway to the present moment. It holds a wisdom that predates language, a truth that cannot be accessed through intellect alone. The work is not to force the body into submission, but to learn its language, to listen to its subtle cues, and to befriend its long-held tensions.

For those dealing with fatigue from chronic stress, consider Thorne Vitamin B Complex (paid link).

The path back to this internal home is not a straight line, but a spiral, circling the same core wounds, the same tender places, but each time from a slightly higher vantage point, with a little more light, a little more perspective. It is the slow, courageous work of a lifetime. It is the quiet devotion of turning toward yourself, again and again, with a kindness that was never offered but is now, finally, available. It is the discovery that beneath the armor, beneath the shattered pieces, there has always been an unbreakable wholeness. You are not a problem to be solved. You are a process to be witnessed.
